title: CERN Researcher — Particle Physics
setting: CERN, the European Organisation for Nuclear Research, Geneva, Switzerland
companySize: Experiment collaboration of 3,000+ physicists; CERN itself employs 2,500 staff
description: You are a postdoctoral researcher on one of the large LHC experiments (ATLAS or CMS). Your work involves analysing collision data from the world's most powerful particle accelerator, developing analysis software, maintaining detector hardware, and contributing to one of the largest scientific collaborations in history. You live in Geneva or nearby France and work in a truly international environment.
salary: $85,000–$120,000 AUD equivalent (CERN fellow stipend, tax-free in Switzerland)

schedule.activity: Arrive at CERN, check the LHC status and overnight data quality
schedule.note: The LHC ran overnight — 40 million collisions per second, and your detector captured a fraction

schedule.activity: Analysis work — run your signal selection code on the latest dataset
schedule.note: Searching for evidence of a rare decay process in billions of events

schedule.activity: Physics analysis meeting — present your latest results to the working group
schedule.note: 50 people in the room, all experts. Expect tough questions.

schedule.activity: Debug a detector calibration issue in the calorimeter
schedule.note: Detector operations shifts are a core responsibility — everyone takes turns

schedule.activity: Lunch at CERN Restaurant 1 — sit with physicists from 5 different countries
schedule.note: The most international workplace on Earth — 110+ nationalities

schedule.activity: Develop a new machine learning classifier for signal-background separation
schedule.note: ML is increasingly central to particle physics analysis

schedule.activity: Collaboration-wide meeting — status update on the experiment's physics programme
schedule.note: 3,000 collaborators; most join remotely from universities worldwide

schedule.activity: Work on the internal review of a paper headed for publication
schedule.note: CERN papers go through extensive internal review before journal submission

schedule.activity: Wrap up, push code to GitLab, update analysis documentation
schedule.note: Reproducibility is taken extremely seriously in big collaborations

schedule.activity: Cycle home along Lake Geneva — discuss physics with a colleague on the ride
schedule.note: The views from CERN are spectacular. Mont Blanc on a clear day.

responsibilities: Analyse LHC collision data to search for new physics phenomena
Develop and maintain analysis software and frameworks
Take detector operation shifts (monitoring, calibration, data quality)
Contribute to detector hardware upgrades and maintenance
Present results at collaboration meetings and international conferences
Write and review papers for the collaboration
Mentor PhD students working on the experiment
challenges: The sheer scale of the collaboration — your analysis is one of hundreds
Navigating the politics of a 3,000-person collaboration
The pressure to find new physics when the data might show nothing new
Living abroad, far from family (most CERN researchers are expatriates)
The uncertainty of short-term contracts (CERN fellowships are typically 2–3 years)
rewards: Working at the frontier of human knowledge — literally the edge of the Standard Model
Being part of historic discoveries (Higgs boson, gravitational waves of the particle world)
Collaborating with the brightest physicists from around the globe
Access to the most powerful scientific instruments ever built
Living in one of the most beautiful parts of Europe
careerPath: PhD → CERN Fellow (2–3 years) → Staff physicist or university faculty → Senior physicist → Group leader
